subject: Science
overall_learning_objectives: Understand the unique nature of carbon and its ability to form bonds
Identify the difference between saturated and unsaturated hydrocarbons
Recognize the various allotropes of carbon
Understand the basic naming of simple carbon compounds
Identify common carbon-based substances in daily life
Recognize the importance of soap and detergents in cleaning

lesson_plan.title: Bonding in Carbon: The Covalent Bond
teaching_strategy: Analogy: Sharing of snacks to represent electron sharing
Visual aids showing the atomic structure of Carbon
Drawing simple molecules like $H_{2}$, $O_{2}$, and $CH_{4}$
student_activities: Draw electron-dot structures for Hydrogen and Chlorine
List 5 items used since morning that contain carbon

lesson_plan.title: Allotropes of Carbon
teaching_strategy: Show and Tell: A diamond ring (image) vs. pencil lead (graphite)
Comparison table of physical properties
3D models of Diamond and Graphite lattices
student_activities: Touch and feel graphite from a pencil
List the uses of diamond and graphite based on their properties

lesson_plan.title: Versatile Nature of Carbon
topics_covered: Straight chains, branched chains, and rings
teaching_strategy: Building block analogy (Lego) for catenation
Drawing structures of Methane, Ethane, and Ethene
Visual charts of different chain types
student_activities: Identify saturated vs. unsaturated bonds in given drawings

lesson_plan.title: Homologous Series and Naming
teaching_strategy: Family analogy for the homologous series
Step-by-step guide to naming the first four alkanes
Flashcards for naming practice
student_activities: Calculate the difference in molecular formula between $CH_{4}$ and $C_{2}H_{6}$
Name the compound with the formula $C_{3}H_{8}$

lesson_plan.title: Chemical Properties: Combustion and Oxidation
topics_covered: Burning of carbon and its compounds
teaching_strategy: Demonstration: Lighting a candle vs. a Bunsen burner (Blue vs. Yellow flame)
Discussion on why gas stoves have holes for air
Simple word equations for combustion
student_activities: Observe the bottom of a vessel used on a wood fire vs. a gas stove
assessment: Short answer: Why does coal burn without a flame?
Exit slip: What are the products of combustion?

lesson_plan.title: Ethanol and Ethanoic Acid
teaching_strategy: Displaying a bottle of vinegar and hand sanitizer
Discussion on why drinking alcohol is harmful
Simple physical properties (smell, state)
student_activities: Smell vinegar and record the observation
List two uses for Ethanol in daily life
assessment: Identifying the acid present in vinegar

lesson_plan.title: Soaps and Detergents
topics_covered: Structure of soap molecules (Head and Tail)
How soap removes dirt (Micelle formation - simple)
Difference between soap and detergent (hard water context)
teaching_strategy: Drawing a 'Tadpole' shape for soap molecules
Demonstration: Mixing oil and water with and without soap
Visual aid showing a Micelle
student_activities: Draw a simple soap molecule and label the water-loving head
Observe the 'lather' formation in a soap solution

teacher_tip: Carbon chemistry can be abstract. Use lots of 'ball-and-stick' models or even clay and toothpicks so students can physically build the 4-bond structure of carbon. This makes the concept of 'tetravalency' much easier to grasp.
